query: most common symptom of aortic-iliac occlusive disease

retrieved_docs.title: Aortoiliac Occlusive Disease -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Recognizing the numerous risk factors for AIOD allows clinicians to implement medical therapies that alleviate symptoms and extend patient survival. The advent of prosthetic graft materials in the 1960s revolutionized the surgical treatment of AIOD, offering a viable solution for patients with significant aortic and iliac occlusions. This review will examine the pathophysiology, clinical manifestations, diagnostic approaches, and contemporary management options, focusing on surgical and endovascular interventions to improve outcomes in patients with AIOD.

retrieved_docs.title: Aortoiliac Occlusive Disease -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Aortoiliac occlusive disease (AIOD), is a variant of peripheral artery disease (PAD) that affects the infrarenal aorta and iliac arteries, obstructing blood flow to distal organs through narrowed arterial lumens or embolization of plaques. Like other arterial diseases, this condition can present a wide range of symptoms, from asymptomatic cases to limb-threatening emergencies. For many patients, collateral circulation can be sufficient to manage symptoms nonoperatively. However, obstructive lesions are commonly found in the infrarenal aorta, common iliac artery, internal iliac (hypogastric) artery, external iliac artery, or combinations of these vessels.

retrieved_docs.title: Aortoiliac Occlusive Disease -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: A subset of AIOD patients may present with Leriche syndrome, a triad of symptoms including buttock claudication, erectile dysfunction, and absent femoral pulses. [10] This syndrome, named after Dr René Leriche, a French surgeon, indicates severe aortoiliac occlusion. [11] In more advanced cases, patients may seek emergent care due to complications from severe stenosis or acute embolism, leading to chronic limb-threatening ischemia (CLTI). [12] CLTI is defined by rest pain, gangrene, or lower limb ulceration lasting more than 2 weeks, indicating critical arterial insufficiency in the affected limb. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Aortoiliac Occlusive Disease -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: A thorough medical history helps distinguish AIOD from other vascular or musculoskeletal conditions. At the same time, a detailed physical examination, including pulse assessments and inspection of the lower extremities for ulcerations or gangrene, is crucial for determining the presence of ischemia and planning treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: External Iliac Arteries -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Stenosis or occlusion of the iliofemoral arterial system restricts the flow of blood to the lower extremity. Depending on the patient's activity level, those with arterial occlusive disease of the external iliac artery may have no symptoms or pain in the lower extremity muscles with walking. The most common disease-causing external iliac artery occlusive disease is atherosclerosis. Fibromuscular dysplasia can also cause external iliac artery stenosis and should be considered in symptomatic patients who do not have cardiovascular risk factors.

retrieved_docs.title: Aortoiliac Occlusive Disease -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: AIOD is a PAD variant affecting the lower aorta and iliac arteries. Similar to PAD, AIOD is typically caused by progressive atherosclerosis, characterized by the buildup of plaques composed of lipids, cholesterol, calcium, and other substances within the arterial walls. These plaques can obstruct blood flow to the lower extremities, pelvis, and abdominal organs, leading to stenosis and eventual occlusion of the infrarenal aorta, common iliac, internal iliac (hypogastric), or external iliac arteries.

retrieved_docs.title: Aortoiliac Occlusive Disease -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: In AIOD, this narrowing typically occurs in the infrarenal aorta, common iliac arteries, or external iliac arteries. Initially, collateral circulation may compensate for reduced blood flow, allowing some patients to remain asymptomatic. However, as atherosclerosis advances and these compensatory mechanisms fail, symptoms like claudication, rest pain, and critical limb ischemia emerge. Furthermore, the plaques may rupture or dislodge, leading to distal embolization and exacerbating ischemic symptoms. If untreated, this progressive ischemia can result in tissue loss and potentially limb amputation. Nonatherosclerotic causes, such as large vessel vasculitis like Takayasu arteritis, can also contribute to similar vascular occlusion and ischemic consequences.

retrieved_docs.title: Aortoiliac Occlusive Disease -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Obtaining a medical history and performing a physical examination are critical in assessing disease severity and guiding management. Patients commonly present with cramping pain in the lower extremities that occurs during and after physical activity and is relieved by rest. This classic symptom, claudication, is caused by reduced muscle blood flow due to arterial stenosis. The location of the muscle cramping can help determine the severity and extent of the stenosis. More proximal symptoms, such as buttock or thigh claudication, typically suggest more significant or higher-level occlusions, often involving the aortoiliac region.

retrieved_docs.title: Leriche Syndrome(Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: AIOD is classified as Type I when confined to the distal abdominal aorta and common iliac arteries, Type II when predominately distal abdominal aorta with disease extension into common iliac and external iliac arteries, and Type III when affecting the aortoiliac segment and femoropopliteal vessels. [2] AIOD, when symptomatic, classically presents with a triad of claudication, impotence, and absence of femoral pulses. Claudication refers to cramping leg pain reproducible with exercise.

retrieved_docs.title: Aortoiliac Occlusive Disease -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Aortoiliac occlusive disease (AIOD) is a form of peripheral artery disease affecting the infrarenal aorta and iliac arteries, reducing blood flow to the lower extremities. Commonly caused by atherosclerosis, AIOD can present with symptoms ranging from claudication to limb-threatening ischemia, depending on the severity and extent of arterial occlusion. Risk factors include diabetes, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, smoking, and age. Diagnosis often involves noninvasive testing, such as the ankle-brachial index, and advanced imaging techniques. Management includes medical therapies, lifestyle modifications, and interventional procedures like endovascular stenting or aortofemoral bypass to restore blood flow and improve long-term outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Infrainguinal Occlusive Disease -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Infra inguinal occlusive diseases are amongst the commonest vascular diseases, worldwide. This activity outlines the evaluation, treatment and management of patients suffering from the disease and highlights the role of the inter-professional team in improving care for patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Aortoiliac Occlusive Disease -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Evaluating AIOD involves a comprehensive approach, integrating clinical history, physical examination, and diagnostic testing to confirm the presence and severity of arterial obstruction. In patients with multiple vascular risk factors, such as claudication and absent femoral pulses, the diagnosis is often straightforward. Still, some may have palpable pedal pulses at rest due to strong collateral circulation, making thorough history-taking crucial.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Aorta -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The aorta is known for certain pathology including aortic aneurysms, dissection, coarctation, aortitis, and atherosclerotic disease. Ascending aortic aneurysms and dissections involve surgical management. Descending thoracic and abdominal aneurysms involve medical management until the vessel reaches a size greater than 50% of its baseline diameter at which point they require surgical placement of stents or grafts. Smoking, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia are strong risk factors. Aortic coarctation is often diagnosed within the first few years of life. Blood pressures are often higher in the upper limbs as opposed to the lower limbs. Severe cases can lead to lower limb cyanosis. Collateral flow via the intercostal arteries provides nutrient-rich blood to the abdomen and distal extremities. This condition is usually managed surgically. Aortitis may result from an infectious etiology such as syphilis or an autoimmune origin such as arteritis. Depending on the cause, the aorta may dilate or narrow. Atherosclerotic disease may lead to peripheral artery disease and predispose individuals to dissection or aneurysm. However, plaques may simply decrease the luminal radius, leading to distal ischemia. Leriche syndrome, or aortoiliac occlusive disease, is a notable atherosclerotic disease affecting the distal abdominal aorta and iliac arteries. [20] [21] [22] [23] ] [24] [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Aortoiliac Occlusive Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Surgical and Endovascular Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical and endovascular revascularization techniques include open and minimally invasive techniques, each with distinct advantages based on patient conditions. [1] [34] [35] They play a pivotal role in treating AIOD, offering options to restore blood flow and alleviate symptoms like claudication and critical limb ischemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Aortoiliac Occlusive Disease -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify patients at risk for aortoiliac occlusive disease through clinical history and risk factor assessment. Differentiate between aortoiliac occlusive disease and other causes of lower extremity pain, such as musculoskeletal or neurologic conditions. Select the optimal treatment modality, balancing patient comorbidities and disease extent. Collaborate with vascular surgeons, radiologists, and other specialists for comprehensive management. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Internal Iliac Arteries -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The internal iliac arteries are at risk of various pathologies that impact the arterial system. Internal iliac artery stenosis is a relatively common occurrence that is often due to atherosclerosis. This condition is typically associated with aortic or common iliac artery stenosis. [20] Isolated occlusion of the internal iliac arteries may occur, although it is less common and more challenging to diagnose than aortoiliac-associated disease. [21] The rich collateral circulation of the branches of the internal iliac artery hep to bypass arterial obstructions resulting from surgery, trauma, or arterial disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 829
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis is identified as a pathological finding within the aorta abdominalis, indicating that atherosclerotic disease commonly involves the abdominal segment of the aorta. The aorta abdominalis itself is linked to peripheral vascular bypass, reflecting that surgical bypass procedures are often considered for restoring blood flow when significant aortic disease is present. In the lower extremity, the arteria femoralis (femoral artery) may present with لغط فخذي (thigh bruit), a clinical sign that suggests turbulent flow or stenosis in the femoral vessels. The femoral artery is also a direct procedural site for peripheral vascular bypass, demonstrating that bypass surgery can be performed on the femoral artery to circumvent occlusive lesions. Documentation of peripheral vascular bypass is associated with a surgical history that is relevant to physical‑therapy treatment, encompassing details such as the type of surgery, timing, and patient identifiers. Together, these relationships illustrate how atherosclerotic disease of the abdominal aorta and femoral artery can lead to clinical findings (e.g., thigh bruit) and necessitate peripheral vascular bypass surgery, with the procedure’s history being integral to multidisciplinary patient management, including physical therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1787
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis is a disease that manifests in the aorta abdominalis, indicating that the abdominal segment of the aorta is the typical site where atherosclerotic plaques develop. Because the aorta abdominalis is a major conduit for blood flow to the lower extremities, it is also a focal point in the planning and execution of peripheral vascular bypass procedures, which are performed to restore adequate circulation when peripheral arterial disease compromises blood supply. Recognizing the presence of abdominal aortic atherosclerosis and its impact on the aorta abdominalis is therefore essential for clinicians when evaluating patients for peripheral vascular bypass surgery, as it influences both diagnostic assessment and surgical strategy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2533
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis is identified at the aorta abdominalis, indicating that the disease process is located within the abdominal aorta. The aorta abdominalis is linked to peripheral vascular bypass, reflecting that bypass procedures may be performed in relation to this segment of the aorta. The arteria femoralis is associated with the clinical finding of لغط فخذي (femoral bruit), a sign that can suggest turbulent flow or arterial disease in the thigh region. Additionally, the arteria femoralis serves as a direct procedural site for peripheral vascular bypass, meaning that the femoral artery is commonly used as an access or conduit vessel during bypass surgery. Together, these connections underscore the relevance of both the abdominal aorta and femoral artery in the assessment and surgical management of peripheral vascular disease, with atherosclerotic involvement of the abdominal aorta and audible femoral bruit guiding the planning of bypass interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2116
retrieved_docs.content: The arteria femoralis (femoral artery) is clinically associated with the finding site described as لغط فخذي, which corresponds to a thigh bruit. Detection of a bruit over the thigh indicates turbulent blood flow within the femoral artery and can be a useful physical examination sign for evaluating vascular conditions such as arterial stenosis, aneurysm, or peripheral arterial disease in the lower extremity. Recognizing this relationship aids clinicians in localizing vascular abnormalities and guiding further diagnostic or therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 184
retrieved_docs.content: and visceral atherosclerosis and is linked to aortic calcification تكلس أبهري, increased risk for ineffective cerebral tissue perfusion, and may be visualized by aortic imaging (صورة الأبهر). The aorta and its abdominal segment (aorta abdominalis) are direct procedural sites for aortic resection, replacement, anastomosis, and peripheral vascular bypass, the latter also involving the femoral artery (arteria femoralis), where a thigh bruit لغط فخذي may be detected on clinical examination. Documentation of peripheral vascular bypass appears in surgical histories and is associated with multidisciplinary care such as physical‑therapy‑related treatment. Collectively, these relationships illustrate the central role of the heart and aorta in rheumatic valve disease, systemic atherosclerotic disease, cerebral perfusion risk, and a range of cardiovascular diagnostic and therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1513
retrieved_docs.content: التصلب العصيدي is a vascular condition that is clinically associated with several observable signs and health considerations. It is linked to the presence of a systolic murmur (النفخة الانقباضية), indicating turbulent blood flow during cardiac contraction, and to arterial bruits such as a carotid bruit (لغط الشريان السباتي) and a femoral bruit (لغط فخذي), which reflect turbulent flow in the carotid and femoral arteries respectively. Patients with التصلب العصيدي may also present with a limp (عرج), suggesting compromised limb perfusion or musculoskeletal involvement, and the condition emphasizes the importance of routine health maintenance (حفظ الصحة الروتيني) to monitor and manage cardiovascular risk. Additionally, التصلب العصيدي is associated with ذَبْحَةُ برنزميتال, a specific clinical or pathological finding that further characterizes the disease spectrum. The condition includes a more extensive form known as التصلب العصيدي المعمم, representing a generalized manifestation of arterial hardening. Recognizing these interrelated clinical features and the broader subtype aids clinicians in comprehensive assessment, early detection, and targeted management of patients with arterial sclerosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 200
retrieved_docs.content: Arteriografie functions as the overarching category for imaging of the femoral and lower‑extremity arterial tree, encompassing the contrast arteriogram of the femoral and lower extremity arteries and its inversely defined sub‑type, the contrast aortogram. Digital subtraction angiography (DSA) of the aorta and lower limb is linked to both the femoral‑lower‑extremity contrast arteriogram and the contrast aortogram; DSA protocols with 4–6, 7–9, ≤3, or ≥10 acquisition runs are classified as “other” for the femoral‑lower‑extremity study but as “primary” for the contrast aortogram, with unilateral ≤3‑run studies also mapped to the Arabic descriptor “صورة الأبهر” (aortic image). The femoral artery (arteria femoralis) is explicitly associated with the femoral‑lower‑extremity contrast arteriogram, underscoring its central role in peripheral vascular imaging. The abdominal aorta (aorta abdominalis) is directly related to peripheral vascular bypass surgery, in which the femoral artery serves as the operative site for creating the bypass to restore flow in compromised peripheral vessels. Clinical examination of the femoral artery may reveal a thigh bruit (لغط فخذي), a sign of turbulent flow suggestive of stenosis or occlusion that often leads to consideration of revascularization. Documentation of the peripheral vascular bypass appears in the patient’s surgical history and is linked to subsequent physical‑therapy‑related care, specifying the procedure type and timing within the overall treatment plan. Together, these entities and relationships define a structured framework for categorizing aortic and lower‑extremity angiographic studies, guiding protocol selection, accurate reporting, and multidisciplinary management of peripheral arterial disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 193
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis is diagnosed by findings in the abdominal aorta (aorta abdominalis). The abdominal aorta also serves as the proximal vessel for peripheral vascular bypass, a procedure that may involve the femoral artery (arteria femoralis) as the direct operative site and is associated with thigh bruit (لغط فخذي). Lower‑extremity arterial occlusive disease is treated by embolectomy and endarterectomy of the femoropopliteal artery, which are classified as incisions of blood vessels, a subcategory of incision, excision, and occlusion of vessels, and belong to the broader group of operative procedures on arteries of the extremity and vascular surgical procedures on the lower limb. The therapeutic aim of these arterial interventions is blood flushing (إراقة الدم) to restore flow. In the venous system, the internal rectal venous plexus is the anatomic site of hemorrhoids, described as unspecified hemorrhoids without complications, and is the target of hemorrhoidal ligation

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2079
retrieved_docs.content: The aorta abdominalis is directly related to peripheral vascular bypass, serving as the primary inflow source or conduit in surgical procedures that bypass occluded peripheral arteries to restore adequate blood flow. This connection is essential for planning and executing revascularization strategies in patients with peripheral arterial disease.

retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis is identified as a finding in the abdominal aorta (aorta abdominalis) and is classified under peripheral and visceral atherosclerosis (تكلس أبهري), which serves as a parent condition for التصلب العصيدي (atherosclerosis). This atherosclerotic process, also referred to as senile arteritis, is linked to coronary atherosclerosis (تصلب عصيدي للطعم المجازي في الشريان التاجي الفطري) and underlies many cardiovascular diseases, including chronic unspecified ischemic heart disease (مرض قلبي إقفاري مزمن، غير محدد) within the Blood, Heart and Circulation domain. Clinical manifestations of التصلب العصيدي include ذَبْحَةُ برنزميتال, النفخة الانقباضية (systolic murmur), حفظ الصحة الروتيني (routine health maintenance), عرج (limp), لغط الشريان السباتي (carotid bruit), لغط فخذي (femoral bruit), and مخطط كهربية القلب غير طبيعي (abnormal electrocardiogram); a diffuse form (التصلب العصيدي المعمم) is also described. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 145
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis is a pathological finding located in the abdominal aorta (aorta abdominalis) and underscores the need for imaging of the aorta abdominalis when assessing atherosclerotic disease. The abdominal aorta is also linked to peripheral vascular bypass, for which the femoral artery (arteria femoralis) serves as the direct procedural site; clinical examination of the femoral artery may reveal a thigh bruit (لغط فخذي). In renal replacement therapy, an external arteriovenous shunt created by an external cannula is performed for dialysis (غسيل الكلى) and is classified under the creation, revision, or removal of arteriovenous fistulas or vessel‑to‑vessel cannulas for dialysis, including revision of arteriovenous shunts for renal dialysis. These procedures directly involve veins (vény) and are part of the broader category “incision, excision, and occlusion of vessels.” Lower‑extremity arterial interventions are organized hierarchically: embolectomy and endarterectomy

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 827
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis is identified as a pathological finding located in the abdominal aorta. The abdominal aorta is directly involved in peripheral vascular bypass procedures, which are surgical interventions that create an alternative pathway for blood flow (إراقة الدم) through the vascular system. Vessels (vény) are generally associated with peripheral vascular bypass, serving as indirect sites that facilitate the rerouting of blood. The femoral artery (arteria femoralis) is a direct procedural site for peripheral vascular bypass and may present clinically with a femoral bruit (لغط فخذي), indicating turbulent flow at that location. The history of undergoing a peripheral vascular bypass is recorded as part of the patient’s surgical background that is relevant to subsequent physical therapy management. Together, these relationships highlight how atherosclerotic disease of the abdominal aorta, vascular bypass surgery, and associated findings in the femoral artery are interconnected considerations in comprehensive vascular care and rehabilitation planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 2 -- Community 1086
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis is a vascular disease that is identified in the abdominal aorta (aorta abdominalis). The abdominal aorta may be involved in peripheral vascular bypass procedures, which are surgical interventions recorded in a patient’s operative history and are relevant when planning physical therapy. The femoral artery (arteria femoralis) is a common site where peripheral vascular bypass is performed and may present with a thigh bruit (لغط فخذي) on physical examination. Amputation of the lower extremity encompasses several specific procedures, including toe amputation (بتر أصبع القدم), foot amputation (بتر القدم), below‑knee amputation (بتر تحت الركبة), and above‑knee amputation (بتر فوق الركبة). These procedures are documented as part of the surgical history that influences physical‑therapy management. Clinically, lower‑extremity amputation is frequently associated with congestive heart failure (فشل قلبي احتقاني), reflecting the systemic burden of peripheral arterial disease. Complications of lower‑extremity amputation can include necrosis of the amputation stump of the right lower limb, which is morphologically described as النَخَر and is directly linked to the underlying amputation. Recognizing necrotic stump tissue is important for postoperative care and rehabilitation planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1162
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis is a pathological finding located in the aorta abdominalis. Because disease of the aorta abdominalis can compromise peripheral circulation, it is often associated with the need for a peripheral vascular bypass. The peripheral vascular bypass commonly uses the arteria femoralis as a direct procedural site to create an alternate pathway for blood flow. Consequently, a peripheral vascular bypass constitutes an element of the patient’s surgical history that is relevant to the planning and execution of physical‑therapy‑related treatment, as indicated by the associated record of surgical history linked to physical therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 169
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis is a vascular disease localized to the abdominal aorta (aorta abdominalis) and contributes to peripheral vascular disease that may require peripheral vascular bypass surgery. Peripheral vascular bypass frequently involves the femoropopliteal artery (arteria femoralis) and can be associated with a femoral bruit, indicating turbulent blood flow. In renal replacement therapy, creation, revision, or removal of an external arteriovenous shunt or fistula for dialysis utilizes veins as the primary procedural site, linking venous access to dialysis and to peripheral vascular bypass procedures. Venous operations on the lower extremity include varicose vein ligation and stripping and stripping of lower‑limb varicose veins, which target the veins of the lower extremity and fall under the broader category of incision, excision, and occlusion of vessels and vascular surgical procedures on the lower limb. Arterial interventions on the lower limb comprise embolectomy and endarterectomy of the femoropopliteal artery performed through leg incisions; these are classified as incision of blood vessels and represent specific types of lower‑limb vascular surgery that regulate arterial blood flow. The interrelated arterial and venous procedures—affecting the abdominal aorta, femoropopliteal artery, and lower‑limb veins—underscore the integrated role of vascular surgery in managing atherosclerotic disease, dialysis access, chronic venous insufficiency, and overall blood‑flow regulation. Documentation of peripheral vascular bypass often includes surgical history and physical‑therapy‑related treatment details, providing comprehensive patient‑care information.
